lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market synthetic-rubber manufacturers that repeatedly produce customer-specified elastomers or latex under contract or toll-manufacturing relationships for external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive plants, commodity producers selling only their own standard product, internal production units, pure distributors, natural-rubber processors, rubber-product fabricators, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring batch or continuous production for industrial customers, generally priced per pound or shipment under specifications and purchase agreements, with feedstock and energy clauses negotiated contractually.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS definition covers product manufacturing generally, while the fixed screen requires an outsourced-service model; the lens is narrowed to contract and toll producers to avoid mixing commodity product sellers with transferable recurring-service businesses.

a.bridge: BLS reports that production occupations are about half of manufacturing employment and separately describes planning, scheduling, reporting, customer/supplier communication, data analysis, and quality-control work done by production managers. I treat documentation, scheduling, procurement support, laboratory-data interpretation, and exception triage as AI-exposed while leaving physical operation, maintenance, sampling, and safety-critical control largely unexposed. The broad-manufacturing population likely overstates exposed share for capital-intensive synthetic-rubber plants, so the range is biased downward from generic office-task exposure.
a.rationale: Most plant labor is embodied in physical process operation and maintenance, but a meaningful minority of wage-weighted work consists of production records, quality documentation, planning, forecasting, procurement, regulatory reporting, and technical troubleshooting that AI can assist or partially substitute.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from three independent toll or contract synthetic-rubber plants, mapped to O*NET task statements and current automation status, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: BLS evidence is for manufacturing or occupations nationally, not NAICS 325212 contract producers.
The primitive concerns current not-yet-automated tasks; plant-level installed-control-system data were unavailable.

rho.rationale: Within five years, copilots for documentation, planning, quality review, maintenance knowledge, and customer-service work can be deployed, but validated recipes, process safety, cybersecurity, union/workforce practices, and human accountability constrain implementation in live chemical operations.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level before/after studies of deployed AI in batch-record review, laboratory QA, maintenance work orders, and production scheduling, including accepted labor hours and override rates, would materially change this range.
caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than observed adoption.
EPA-described monitoring, recordkeeping, leak-control, and operating requirements make fully autonomous implementation less plausible than back-office assistance.

e.rationale: The Census definition is manufacturing synthetic rubber, not providing contract services. Most firms therefore appear to be product manufacturers; only the minority with repeat tolling or customer-specific production qualifies under the lens.
e.change_evidence: A complete establishment roster coded from company websites, customer contracts, and interviews for third-party toll or contract production would directly identify the eligible share among the injected LMM-band firms.
caveats: No public source measures contract-manufacturing incidence at six digits.
The injected firm count may include integrated or commodity producers that do not fit the recurring outsourced-service lens.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of US employer businesses were age 55 or older in 2018. I translate that broad owner-age pressure into a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability, reducing it because age does not imply a sale and synthetic-rubber plants may be corporate, family-transferred, shut, or absorbed internally. The all-industry owner population likely overstates succession-driven sale incidence for capital-intensive chemical manufacturing.
s5.rationale: Owner age supports meaningful succession pressure, but qualifying transactions are less frequent than retirement events and specialized plants face environmental, technical, and financing diligence that can prevent a transfer.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, or GF Data transactions for US specialty polymer and synthetic-rubber manufacturers under $10M EBITDA, paired with ownership and succession interviews, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The owner-age statistic covers all employer businesses and responding owners, not NAICS 325212 or LMM firms.
It does not observe control transfers or distinguish sales from family succession and closure.

q.rationale: Specification-qualified supply relationships and switching costs permit some retention of implemented savings, but per-pound pricing, feedstock escalators, rebids, renewal negotiation, and globally traded substitutes share gains with customers over five years.
q.change_evidence: A sample of tolling agreements and customer purchase contracts showing open-book clauses, indexation, renewal cadence, cost-down commitments, and bid outcomes would materially change the retained-benefit estimate.
caveats: No contract sample was publicly available, so the range is judgmental.
Product mix and customer concentration can create much stronger or weaker pricing power than the base case.

d5.bridge: The Federal Reserve industrial-production index directly measures aggregate US NAICS 325212 output, not constant-quality demand for outsourced contract production. Monthly index values fell from the mid-to-high 80s in 2021-22 to roughly 70 in 2024, then recovered to roughly 80-86 during 2025-26. I use this volatile, weak trend as the demand anchor and allow broad cyclical outcomes. Domestic production can understate demand when imports gain share, so the proxy may bias demand downward.
d5.rationale: Synthetic rubber remains a necessary industrial input for tires and other elastomer applications, but domestic output has been volatile and below its 2017 base. A near-flat five-year constant-price quantity path is more defensible than extrapolating either the 2023-24 decline or 2025 recovery.
d5.change_evidence: Customer-level five-year volume forecasts by elastomer family, US apparent-consumption data including trade, and contracted toll-production backlog would materially change this range.
caveats: Industrial production is supply-side output, not lens-specific demand.
The index mixes commodity and customer-specific production and does not hold product quality constant.

o.rationale: Year-five elastomer quantity still requires a legally and operationally accountable manufacturer to run polymerization, storage, wastewater, emissions, and quality systems. Software can reduce labor and improve control but cannot self-serve the physical material or remove plant accountability.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers insourcing qualified elastomer production, material substitution eliminating synthetic rubber, or fully autonomous certified chemical plants would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Operator requirement does not guarantee that an independent LMM operator retains the work; large integrated suppliers can consolidate it.
The estimate separates physical operator necessity from labor automation, which is captured in a and rho.

narrative.executive_view: The qualifying opportunity is a narrow contract-manufacturing subset of a capital-intensive product industry. AI can improve planning, quality records, maintenance knowledge, and compliance workflows, but it does not displace the physical or accountable plant operator, and commodity-style pricing limits how much benefit remains with the supplier.
narrative.ai_impact: Useful applications are production scheduling, batch and laboratory record review, root-cause assistance, maintenance work-order drafting, procurement analysis, customer documentation, and regulatory reporting. Human review and deterministic process controls remain necessary around recipes, emissions, quality release, and process safety.
narrative.value_capture: Customer qualification and plant-specific know-how create switching friction, supporting partial capture. Against that, per-unit bids, renewal negotiation, index-linked inputs, and cost-down expectations can transfer much of an efficiency gain to customers over five years.
narrative.firm_availability: Only toll or contract producers meet the recurring outsourced-service lens; most establishments in the NAICS definition simply manufacture synthetic rubber as a product. Broad owner-age evidence suggests succession pressure, but specialized-asset and environmental diligence can lead to internal transfers, strategic sales, or closures rather than clean LMM control transactions.
narrative.demand_durability: Synthetic rubber remains physically required in tires and a range of industrial products, so software does not eliminate the service basket. Federal Reserve output data nevertheless show a long volatile decline followed by partial recovery, making flat rather than robust demand the prudent base case.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the very small eligible contract-production share, absent plant-level task data, unknown contract pass-through terms, environmental liabilities, customer concentration, feedstock volatility, import competition, and whether the injected LMM firm estimate captures truly independent transferable plants.
narrative.principal_driver: Persistent physical demand plus AI-assisted quality, planning, maintenance, and compliance workflows in repeat qualified production.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most of NAICS 325212 is product manufacturing rather than an outsourced service, leaving a poorly measured eligible-firm subset.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 325212 Synthetic Rubber Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments primarily engaged in manufacturing synthetic rubber and distinguishes it from rubber-product manufacturing.

sources.title: Producing the goods of the future: Job opportunities in manufacturing
sources.supports: Reports that production occupations represented about half of manufacturing employment in 2024 and describes projected manufacturing openings.

sources.title: Industrial Production Managers
sources.supports: Lists production-manager duties including scheduling, customer and supplier communication, hiring, production-data analysis, reports, safety monitoring, process streamlining, and quality-control oversight.

sources.title: Regulatory Impact Analysis for Synthetic Organic Chemical Manufacturing and Polymers and Resins Rules
sources.supports: Describes synthetic-rubber production as monomer synthesis followed by polymerization and identifies equipment leaks, process vents, wastewater, and storage tanks as emission sources.

sources.title: Synthetic Organic Chemical Manufacturing Industry NESHAP
sources.supports: States that applicable owners and operators face control, monitoring, reporting, and recordkeeping requirements for process vents, storage vessels, transfer racks, wastewater streams, and equipment leaks.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census basic statistics: NAICS 325212
sources.supports: Shows a national six-digit industry table for Synthetic Rubber Manufacturing and identifies its universe as operating establishments with paid employees.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages: Over Half of U.S. Business Owners Were Age 55 and Over
sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older in the 2019 ABS, data year 2018.

sources.title: Industrial Production: Synthetic Rubber (NAICS 325212), table data
sources.supports: Provides monthly Federal Reserve industrial-production index observations for NAICS 325212, including the decline through 2024 and partial recovery in 2025-26.

sources.title: EPA air toxic rule for elastomer production
sources.supports: Identifies synthetic-rubber end uses including tires, hoses, belts, footwear, adhesives, caulks, wire insulation, and floor tiles.
